heading: State of mind; bystander liability; control
label: 6612 State of mind; bystander liability; control
notes: References in TextSection 4 of the Year 2000 Information and Readiness Disclosure Act, referred to in subsec. (d), is , which was formerly set out in a note under .section 4 of Pub. L. 105–271section 1 of this title

children.heading: Defendant’s state of mind
children.label: a Defendant’s state of mind
children.text: In a Y2K action other than a claim for breach or repudiation of contract, and in which the defendant’s actual or constructive awareness of an actual or potential Y2K failure is an element of the claim, the defendant is not liable unless the plaintiff establishes that element of the claim by the standard of evidence under applicable State law in effect on the day before .January 1, 1999

children.heading: Limitation on bystander liability for Y2K failures
children.label: b Limitation on bystander liability for Y2K failures
A
children.text: the defendant is not the manufacturer, seller, or distributor of a product, or the provider of a service, that suffers or causes the Y2K failure at issue;

children.label: B
children.text: the plaintiff is not in substantial privity with the defendant; and

children.label: C
children.text: the defendant’s actual or constructive awareness of an actual or potential Y2K failure is an element of the claim under applicable law,

children.heading: Substantial privity
children.label: 2 Substantial privity
children.text: For purposes of paragraph (1)(B), a plaintiff and a defendant are in substantial privity when, in a Y2K action arising out of the performance of professional services, the plaintiff and the defendant either have contractual relations with one another or the plaintiff is a person who, prior to the defendant’s performance of such services, was specifically identified to and acknowledged by the defendant as a person for whose special benefit the services were being performed.

children.heading: Certain claims excluded
children.label: 3 Certain claims excluded
children.text: For purposes of paragraph (1)(C), claims in which the defendant’s actual or constructive awareness of an actual or potential Y2K failure is an element of the claim under applicable law do not include claims for negligence but do include claims such as fraud, constructive fraud, breach of fiduciary duty, negligent misrepresentation, and interference with contract or economic advantage.

children.heading: Control not determinative of liability
children.label: c Control not determinative of liability
children.text: The fact that a Y2K failure occurred in an entity, facility, system, product, or component that was sold, leased, rented, or otherwise within the control of the party against whom a claim is asserted in a Y2K action shall not constitute the sole basis for recovery of damages in that action. A claim in a Y2K action for breach or repudiation of contract for such a failure is governed by the terms of the contract.

children.heading: Protections of the Year 2000 Information and Readiness Disclosure Act apply
children.label: d Protections of the Year 2000 Information and Readiness Disclosure Act apply
children.text: The protections for the exchanges of information provided by section 4 of the Year 2000 Information and Readiness Disclosure Act () shall apply to any Y2K action.Public Law 105–271
